design_philosophy.core_loop: Navigate vertically-scrolling stages while managing weapon loadouts and power-up acquisition to eliminate waves of enemies and defeat stage bosses
design_philosophy.primary_tension: Resource scarcity (lives/continues) balanced against pattern memorization and reaction speed; weapon customization decisions create meaningful strategic risk
design_philosophy.player_fantasy: Pilot an advanced starfighter (Vic Viper) with modular weapon systems through increasingly difficult alien threats; achieve mastery through learned patterns and optimized loadouts
design_philosophy.what_makes_it_opinionated: Arcade version famously unforgiving with no continues and demands perfect pattern execution; introduces Edit Mode allowing players to create custom weapon combinations, shifting from prescribed builds to personalized loadouts. SNES port deliberately softens this stance with continues and additional weapon options, representing a philosophical compromise between arcade purity and accessibility

difficulty.lives_system: Limited reserve ships; arcade version employs strict lives-limited design with no continue feature; SNES port allows continues when all lives exhausted, fundamentally changing difficulty philosophy
difficulty.continues_system: Arcade: no continues, game over on death. SNES: allows continues after losing all lives. This represents core design divergence between versions
difficulty.checkpointing: Stage-based progression; players restart from stage beginning upon death
difficulty.player_agency_in_difficulty: High agency: SNES port includes unlockable 'Arcade' difficulty mode via code input (rapid A-button tapping). Beginner mode in Japanese arcade version eases first three levels. Edit Mode weapon customization allows players to optimize difficulty-managing loadouts. Player can select weapon schemes to match skill level and playstyle

progression.upgrade_system: Power-up system with modular weapon components (missiles, double shots, lasers, shields, special weapons). Edit Mode allows custom combinations. SNES port adds 'Reduce II' protective power-up reducing damage from two hits instead of one
progression.unlock_mechanism: Stage selection unlocked progressively on PS2 port after clearing stages. Arcade Difficulty mode unlocked via input code on SNES. Two hidden stages based on Gradius I and Salamander accessible through gameplay
replayability_hooks: Custom weapon loadout creation via Edit Mode encourages multiple playthroughs with different strategies
Hidden bonus level (Stage 4, third-person perspective navigation) rewards exploration
Two hidden stages reference previous series entries
Arcade Difficulty mode provides significantly harder challenge after SNES completion
Weapon variety encourages experimentation with different pre-defined schemes

world_building_techniques: Enemy design reflects alien nature of Bacterion Empire
Stage environments (crystal worlds, cell stages) create organic/mechanical aesthetic contrast
Vic Viper design and naming establish series continuity and technological context

boss_design.philosophy: Large-scale enemy entities serving as stage-end climaxes; designed as pattern-memorization encounters requiring learned attack sequences. SNES version introduces Beacon boss at conclusion of new high-speed stage
notable_bosses: Beacon (SNES-exclusive, high-speed stage finale, parallels Gradius II structure)

faction_indicators: Bacterion Empire established as player antagonist faction
danger_signals: Arcade version's notorious difficulty level signals hardcore skill requirement
No-continue design in arcade version signals hardcore audience expectation
Slowdown implementation in SNES becomes environmental hazard affecting readability
environmental_storytelling: Stage environments progress from mechanical/industrial to organic/biological (cell stage as final level in SNES version)
Crystal/cube rush stages establish alien/crystalline world aesthetic
Third-person perspective bonus stage (Stage 4) presents spatial anomaly within enemy territory
